summary: Fowler presents two practical approaches for substituting service stubs during testing while keeping real implementations for production. The first approach, from Jeremy Stell-Smith, uses an Abstract Factory with a stack-based implementation that simplifies test setup and teardown. The second approach, from Kraig Parkinson, uses a prototype pattern where individual services can be replaced directly. Both patterns solve the common problem of isolating code under test from external dependencies like persistence layers.
key_points: Abstract Factory pattern with a stack of implementations makes managing test stubs cleaner and easier to set up/tear down
Prototype pattern offers a lighter-weight alternative where individual facades can be swapped for testing
Both approaches use static getInstance() methods as the seam for substituting test doubles
The prototype approach with inline anonymous classes resembles Mock Objects' expectation-setting pattern
Cleanup code can go either in finally blocks within tests or in tearDown methods
money_quotes: A common problem with test-enhanced designs is how to create Service Stubs in test mode while letting the real thing be there for production (and for some tests).
As well as the Abstract Factory capability the test factory also has the nice ability to have a stack of implementations - this makes setup of factories easier.
You can think of this as a lightweight way of doing Mock Objects.
key_insight: Strategic use of factories and prototypes as seams in your design enables clean substitution of service stubs for testing without polluting production code.
